replication_study.hypothesis: Students reporting no motivation for distance learning (operationalized as online_learning_1 == 1) have higher odds of severe psychological distress (K10 total score >= 30) compared to students reporting some motivation, controlling for age and available demographic covariates.

data_plan.source_type: Online self-administered questionnaire (survey responses; deidentified CSV)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Single cross-sectional survey (May 2020 in original paper). This CSV is the provided deidentified dataset; no separate wave identifiers in file.
data_plan.sample_size: 766 records in the deidentified CSV (observed via get_dataset_shape on data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv). Original study sample was 381 (post_registration.json).
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual university students (each row = a respondent)
data_plan.access_details: CSV provided in the replication bundle at data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv. No additional access requests required for this file. (Original paper stated data available from corresponding author; see post_registration.json.)

qualification.explanation: The provided CSV contains the required operational variables to test the focal claim: Kessler_1..Kessler_10 items to compute K10 total and severe category, and online_learning_1 which measures motivation toward distance learning. Thus it is qualified to attempt a direct replication of the claim using independently provided data file. Evidence: column listing includes 'Kessler_1'..'Kessler_10' and 'online_learning_1' (observed in data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv).
qualification.similarity_to_original: Matches original instrument and variables: post_registration.json documents that the original outcome is K10 total and that 'motivation for distance learning' was an independent variable used in multinomial/logistic models. The deidentified CSV includes Kessler_1..10 and online_learning_1, enabling computation of the same outcome and predictor (see file data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv and summary in data/original/22/input/post_registration.json).
qualification.deviation_from_original: Differences making this a distinct replication sample: sample size in provided CSV is 766 (observed) vs 381 in the original paper (post_registration.json cites N=381). This indicates the supplied data are not identical to the exact sample reported in the original paper (possible differences: extended recruitment, combined datasets, or additional records). This provides an independent test of robustness while retaining high fidelity because instrument and variables match. Specific file evidence: data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv (columns and shapes) vs sample_size entry in data/original/22/input/post_registration.json.

data_plan.notes: Observed missingness: Kessler_1..Kessler_10 items have some missing values (e.g., Kessler_1 missing 14, Kessler_10 missing 17) based on dataset summaries; demographic_1 (appears to be birthyear) has many missing values (~134). Column names are generic 'demographic_#' rather than labeled gender/level; mapping to substantive variables will rely on documentation or exploratory checks. The CSV is deidentified and there are two CSVs (one deidentified and one example). Analysis script assumes the survey year 2020 when converting birthyear to age. All IO in the executable script writes/reads from /app/data as required.

steps: 1) Place AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv at /app/data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv inside the execution environment (the provided bundle already contains this file under the study input path).

2) Run the provided Python analysis script: data/original/22/input/replication_data/replicate_analysis__py.py (the script is written to read /app/data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv and to write its output to /app/data/replication_results_AlTammemi.json).
3) Script computes K10 total as the sum of Kessler_1..Kessler_10 (expected response scale 1-5 per item) and creates K10 categories: none (10-19), mild (20-24), moderate (25-29), severe (>=30).
4) Construct outcome variable severe (binary: K10 category == 'severe').

5) Construct primary predictor: motivation for distance learning. In the script I operationalize no_motivation = (online_learning_1 == 1). (If matching the original authors' coding better is desired, code can be inverted to produce 'motivation' variable and results interpreted accordingly.)

6) Create covariates from demographics: derive age from demographic_1 if it appears to be birthyear (survey year 2020 used by default), turn demographic_2/3/4 into categorical dummies and include them as controls where present.
7) Fit logistic regression of severe ~ no_motivation + covariates (with constant). If Logit fails (e.g., separation), use GLM(Binomial) fallback, preserving coefficient estimates and standard errors.
8) Compute coefficient, standard error, p-value, odds ratio and 95% CI for the primary predictor. Save summary statistics, K10 mean, and prevalence to JSON output in /app/data.

9) Compare replication estimates to the original reported effect (OR=0.45 for motivation vs severe distress) and report similarities/differences in effect magnitude, direction, and statistical significance.
planned_method.models: Primary: logistic regression (binary outcome). If the original used multinomial logistic regression to predict distress categories, run that as a secondary analysis; primary replication uses binary severe vs non-severe for direct comparison with the focal OR reported in initial_details.txt.
planned_method.outcome_variable: Binary severe psychological distress (derived from K10 total >= 30, using Kessler_1..Kessler_10 items present in the CSV).
planned_method.independent_variables: Primary: motivation for distance learning (operationalized in script as no_motivation = online_learning_1 == 1). Secondary: alternative codings of online_learning_1 (e.g., treat as ordinal or compare motivated vs not).
planned_method.control_variables: Age (derived from demographic_1 if birthyear-like), demographic_2, demographic_3, demographic_4 (one-hot encoded as available). Additional covariates may be added if further documentation maps demographic_* to specific survey items.
planned_method.tools_software: Python 3.10 (script uses pandas, numpy, statsmodels). Execution environment must have these packages installed.

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Estimate logistic regression coefficient for primary predictor (no_motivation). Report log-odds coefficient and transform to Odds Ratio (exp(coef)), with 95% CI.
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Wald test (z-test) for coefficient significance (p-value) as provided by statsmodels Logit/GLM output. Two-sided tests by default.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Primary approach: listwise deletion for the regression — drop rows missing any K10 item (required to compute K10 total) or missing the primary predictor online_learning_1. For covariates, rows missing those covariates will be dropped if covariates are included. Alternate sensitivity analysis: multiple imputation could be considered if missingness is substantial, but primary replication will use listwise deletion to match the simpler approach reported in many cross-sectional analyses.
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: Only one primary hypothesis test (effect of motivation/no motivation on severe distress). No formal multiple testing correction planned. Secondary analyses (e.g., multinomial model or multiple covariate specifications) will be treated as exploratory.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Primary threshold: two-sided p < 0.05 indicates statistical significance. Directionality: expect that lack of motivation is associated with increased odds of severe distress; equivalently, motivation is associated with decreased odds (original reported OR=0.45 for motivation). Sign and direction of estimated OR will be compared to original (OR=0.45) and magnitude discussed.

files.replicate_analysis__py.py: Python analysis script placed at data/original/22/input/replication_data/replicate_analysis__py.py. It reads /app/data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv (falls back to provided input path), computes K10 total and categories, creates the binary severe outcome and a no_motivation predictor derived from online_learning_1, constructs covariates from demographic_1..4, fits a logistic regression (Logit with GLM Binomial fallback), and writes results summary to /app/data/replication_results_AlTammemi.json. This is the executable entrypoint for replication.
files.AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv: Primary dataset file (data/original/22/input/replication_data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv). Contains columns: session, created, modified, ended, expired, demographic_1..4, Kessler_1..10, online_learning_1, participant_id. The script expects this schema and will raise an error if Kessler_1..10 or online_learning_1 are missing.
files.Analysis.Rmd and Analysis_updated.Rmd (N: R Markdown analysis files included in the original replication_data folder. They are not executed in the Python-based replication pipeline; retained as supplementary material. The RUN POLICY required translation of analysis to Python, which is implemented in replicate_analysis__py.py.

codebase.notes: All I/O in the script is directed to /app/data when possible. The script checks /app/data first and falls back to the provided input path. The script was written to preserve the original logic (K10 scoring, severe threshold, and regression approach) while using Python (pandas, statsmodels). If additional covariate label mapping is required (e.g., mapping demographic_2 to 'gender'), that mapping should be provided or inferred from documentation; currently demographic_* columns are treated generically and one-hot encoded. The script saves output to /app/data/replication_results_AlTammemi.json for easy retrieval.

analysis.instructions: 1) Ensure the provided dataset file is available at /app/data/AlTammemi_Survey_deidentify.csv (container mount or copy). 2) Install dependencies (pip install -r requirements or pip install pandas numpy statsmodels). 3) Run the script: python /app/data/replicate_analysis__py.py (or run the script file located at data/original/22/input/replication_data/replicate_analysis__py.py if working inside the repo, ensuring the working directory has /app/data mounted). 4) Inspect output JSON at /app/data/replication_results_AlTammemi.json. 5) Optionally run multinomial logistic regression as follow-up to reproduce more of the original analysis (original used multinomial logistic regression across K10 categories), using the same predictor set.
analysis.comparison_metrics: Compare: (a) Odds Ratio for the motivation/no-motivation variable (and its 95% CI), (b) p-value for the predictor, (c) direction (OR < 1 or OR > 1) compared to original OR=0.45, (d) magnitude difference in effect size, and (e) prevalence of severe distress and mean K10 total. Report absolute and relative differences and comment on whether the replication supports the original focal claim (same sign, similar magnitude, statistical significance).
